This is especially interesting in the context of Lets Encrypt, where an easy way to manage the renewal is to just have a cron job re-run the `letsencrypt` client every week. One of my servers was failing for an embarrassingly long amount of time, when the cert had expired, but I hadn't restarted the server. I had been in the habit of rebooting the box every few weeks, so I'd never noticed the problem before: it was only when the box stayed up for several months without a reboot that the expired cert was used for too long.

Caching the `stat()` timestamp for a minute sounds fine to me. Given the my server's renewal rate and the letsencrypt cert-expiration time, I could personally handle caching it for up to a week without problems.

The reason they aren't exposed isn't because of dropped globals, but because the module is replacing itself before you gain access to it.

During `import Qt`, Qt.py is read. But once it's done, Qt.py is garbage collected and replaced with whatever we put in `sys.modules`, such as `PySide`.

It's not necessarily a bad idea, but it does make it confusing if we were to add a function or two to the module and expect them to be available like they normally would be.

```python
def reload():
  ...
```

In order for it to be accessible, we would need to attach it to one of the bindings.

```python
def reload():
  ...

PySide.reload = reload
```

Which isn't very obvious I think and might raise questions down the line. Like with `__binding__` which should belong to Qt.py, not a binding itself.

Thanks a lot for the data. One question: how did you check if an alignment is correct? I ask because for mapping quality >=55, minimap2 should barely makes mapping mistakes, but in your table, 1.2% of mapQ>=55 reads are wrong, which is unexpected. My guess is that you are requiring exact coordinate match. However, this usually overestimates the mapping error rate because when there are gaps, the mapping coordinates may be shifted a little. This is particularly true to minimap2/bwa, which may add soft clippings.

With my evaluation script used to draw Figure 1 in [my preprint](https://arxiv.org/abs/1708.01492), I got the following figure below. The mapping error rate in the best mapQ bin is much smaller. You can see the criterions in the figure captions. I can help you to run my script. It is not easy to use, though.

I am not saying my evaluation is better. I just want to understand the differences. Evaluating one's own software is almost always biased. Thanks.

I'm not sure what to do about this one. I can think of lots of different ways to handle it:

1. A separate keyring backend for Mac OS X internet keys.
2. A attribute in the keyring that can be mutated to indicate to use internet keyring.
3. A config parameter that indicates which keyring to use.
4. A separate parameter in the get/set/delete password calls to indicate which variant of the keyring to use.
5. A special indicator parsed from the 'system' that indicates which variant to use.

I don't like (1) at all, and listed it here mostly for completeness. I'm leaning a little bit toward (4), but the main thing that holds me back is that a client that's not aware of the variant wouldn't be able to select it readily.

I believe (5) is akin to the suggestion to use a URL. I'm open to suggestions on this one.

If a user installs `gmaps` system-wide with `pip install gmaps`, this will install the views in his own `.ipython/nbextensions` directory, rather than system-wide.

The temporary solutions are:

> Using contours was the only way that I figured out in how to get them, but if there are other way for that I would be happy to know as well.

The assumption should be that the user has already created their mask via whatever method they choose -- we shouldn't be making that assumption for them.

Instead we should just be looping over the `step` values and then checking to see if the pixel falls inside the `mask`:

```
for x in range(xStart, xEnd, self.step):
    for y in range(yStart, yEnd, self.step):
        if mask is not None and mask[y, x] == 0:
            continue
        kps.append(cv2.KeyPoint(x, y, self.radius))
```

No need to use contours or anything else.

Secondly, I noticed that you're taking the bitwise AND of the mask and image in the GFTT detector:

https://github.com/jrosebr1/imutils/pull/78/files#diff-214d98cb3da7d9232f84590f47b2ab1fR15

I'm not sure why that is being done either. I would do something like:

```
kps = np.argwhere(H > self.T * H.max())
if mask is not None:
    ... loop over all `kps` and then do the check I used above
else:
    kps = [cv2.KeyPoint(pt[1], pt[0], 3) for pt in kps]
```

Here's how I accomplished it:

`git filter-branch --tree-filter 'if [ -f notebook_name.ipynb ]; then nbstripout notebook_name.ipynb; fi'`

The `if` syntax is because if `notebook_name.ipynb` was not checked in from the first commit, then `nbstripout` will fail and the `filter-branch` operation will abort.  Another way to avoid that would be to add an `--ignore-unmatch` flag to `nbstripout`, like `git rm` has, which would be pretty straightforward.

This script is great, by the way.  Now, if only there was a way to get `git diff` to ignore cell output too...

I got the reason why Convolve is so slow in many cases.  
BackpropWeightsScratch/BackpropWeightsScratchLarge are top2 bottlenecks. The reason is that 
there are only 256 threads in total. GPU can not have enough  workload to parallel.  So the current kernel is limited by long latency of sequence to execute batchSize.   not by Texture or ALU speed.   
 It could speedd-up 20x even more by split the shader into 2 kernels: first kernel to execute InputPlane * OutputPlane * batchSize.   The second Kernel to sum batchsize of  InputPlane * OutputPlane. 

    for (int n = 0; n < batchSize; n++) {  // only 256 Threads?  batchSize=128 could have 128 *256 threads. 
        barrier(CLK_LOCAL_MEM_FENCE);
        copyLocal(_imageImage, images + (n * gInputPlanes + upstreamPlane) * gInputSizeSquared, gInputSizeSquared);
        copyLocal(_errorImage, gradOutput + (n * gNumFilters + outPlane) * gOutputSizeSquared, gOutputSizeSquared);
        barrier(CLK_LOCAL_MEM_FENCE);
        if (localId < gFilterSizeSquared) {
            for (int outRow = 0; outRow < gOutputSize; outRow++) {
                int upstreamRow = outRow - gMargin + filterRow;
                for (int outCol = 0; outCol < gOutputSize; outCol++) {
                    const int upstreamCol = outCol - gMargin + filterCol;
                    #define proceed (upstreamRow >= 0 && upstreamCol >= 0 && upstreamRow < gInputSize && upstreamCol < gInputSize)
                    if (proceed) {
                        // these defines reduce register pressure, compared to const

                        #define resultIndex (outRow * gOutputSize + outCol)

                        #define upstreamDataIndex (upstreamRow * gInputSize + upstreamCol)

                        thiswchange += upstreamResult * error;

## Missing NULL item

Same as above. ``LASFile.read(null_subs=True)`` will have no effect, because the method doesn't know what a null value is represented by (until #49 and #118 are finished). There won't be an issue with writing, because there will be no ``np.nan`` values in the data array.

You can add a NULL HeaderItem manually to get things working again on writing the output, although it won't "re-read" the file after you've added the HeaderItem.

```diff
index 91a5ef68..103f6edc 100644
--- a/py3status/modules/file_status.py
+++ b/py3status/modules/file_status.py
@@ -47,6 +47,11 @@ from os.path import basename, expanduser
 
 ERR_NO_PATH = 'no path given'
 
+try:
+    basestring
+except NameError:
+    basestring = str
+
 
 class Py3status:
     """
@@ -79,7 +84,7 @@ class Py3status:
     def post_config_hook(self):
         if self.path:
             # backward compatibility, str to list
-            if isinstance(self.path, str):
+            if isinstance(self.path, basestring):
                 self.path = [self.path]
             # expand user paths
             self.path = list(map(expanduser, self.path))
```

How to create and add entries to list objects which have been defined in the class bindings file which pyangbind creates.

This was something that I’ve struggled a little with - as such, what you want to do is to have the equivalent to:

d = {}
d['foo'] = some_new_obj
but because d[‘foo’] is a particular class instance in this case, we need some way to be able to initialise the entry, such that it can be manipulated. To me, it seemed like having .add(key) and .delete(key) were probably the best names - because it’s not really the same as .append() on a normal list. A Python dict doesn’t have a analogous method that one could steal the name from.

Hopefully the packaging will make the import more logical in the future :-)

Hi @bwaybandit , I'm glad you're liking PyRestTest,

The answer to this will depend on where the string inputs are coming from.  Do they come from previous requests?  If so, an extractor is the way to go, and a template in the headers for the output.  

There's an extractor example here: 
https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/extensions.md#extractors-now-things-get-a-bit-more-complex

You get access to both the request body (as raw bytes, in Python 2 a 'str' object, in Python 3 a 'bytes' object), and the headers - as a list of (key,value) tuples. The latter is because duplicate headers with the same name are allowed, per the HTTP spec.  

If you use headers, make sure to set the 'is_header_extractor' field to true, so that the framework is aware to generate headers for consumption.  

Actual working examples of extractor extensions are here:
https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/tree/master/pyresttest/ext

And of course the main ones here: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/validators.py

If you're using user-supplied data to generate the token value, what you want to register is a custom GENERATOR.  The first link has a section on generators, and there are examples in the main codebase here: https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/generators.py

Does that answer your question?
Cheers,
Sam

I'm afraid storing the type consistently (for all numpy scalar types) is not possible without extreme amounts of hacking around the standard implementation. In Python 3 it might be possible with dynamically swapping `__instancecheck__` (also pretty hacky), but that's not available in Python 2.

Encoding only the types that don't overlap with Python types seems very inconsistent and will probably lead to bugs for users.

So I think the "best" (least bad) course of action is to encode all numpy types as the closest Python type. This means that if you serialize a Numpy `float32`, you'll get back a Python `float`.

I'll add it as an experimental feature for now, feedback is welcome.

Thanks for your PR.

However, I don't quite see the significance of your patch due to the following reasons:

1. **Rare use case**. You are simply uploading torrents by opening a torrent from URL and passing to the API the file-like object on-the-fly. Since this API **mimics a minimal client** (much like a browser), most files to be uploaded are on-disk files. Think of you uploading a file through your web browser; you will choose the file from your filesystem from a window prompted by the browser.
2. **Compatibility issue**. Although you deal a little with this by checking the *file* either to be a string or file-like object, but that is only at the `add_task_bt ` level.  There are still applications that use `upload` and `_req_upload`. 
3. **No feature enhancement**. Using file-like object may gain some performance improvement, but it is bug-prone because the stream's position indicator is handled by users. We want to the postpone the opening of a stream as much as possible to prevent such circumstances as passing an "already-read" file object. Also, having *file* argument treated as either string or file-like object looks anti-pattern and makes the interface more complex.

FYI, you may want to check out [bencode](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/bencode) that converts a torrent into a magnet URI. Then you can pass the magnet URI to `add_task_url`.

It's hard to make everyone happy. The only safe way to access Operation(s) is:
 - via JSON pointer
 - providing operationId

I think a better way for you is to build a customised reducer to collect those Operation(s), with your own key format. Below is an runnable script to demonstrate how to do it

```python
from pyswagger import SwaggerApp, scan
from pyswagger.spec.v2_0.objects import Operation

class MyReducer(object):
    class Disp(scan.Dispatcher): pass

    def __init__(self):
        self.op = {}

    @Disp.register([Operation])
    def _op(self, path, obj, _):
        obj.operationId # operationId
        obj.tags # tags
        self.op[path] = obj # path to obj (json point)

app = SwaggerApp.create("./pyswagger/tests/data/v2_0/wordnik") # path to your document

reducer = MyReducer()
scanner = scan.Scanner(app)
scanner.scan(route=[reducer], root=app.root)
print reducer.op
```

P.S. one drawback of this approach is that: SwaggerApp.root would always be the latest version of Swagger, which means its properties might be changed when Swagger has new version.

You're right. It currently is possible to add a `BootstrapContainer` as a child of another `BootstrapContainer`, `BootstrapRow` or `BootstrapColumn`.
This is a bug.

Unfortunately the parent-children relationship code in django-CMS is very static. This means that all possible relationships are determined during bootstrapping the app.

I think there's some leeway in the spec regarding this. From https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/hh352638(v=exchg.140).aspx:

```
    If the redirection URL is valid, the client tries the URL and then does one of the following:
        If the attempt succeeds, the client proceeds to step 5.
        If the attempt fails, the client proceeds to step 4.
    If the redirection URL is not valid, the client proceeds to step 4.
```

where step 4 is getting the canonical name from DNS. There is no mention of how to decide if a redirection URL is valid.

Looking at the current code, we risk looking up the canonical name from `www.example.com` which is always going to be a bad idea. So something needs to be changed. But if I read your code correctly, it always fails if the redirect hostname starts wih 'www', which I think is incorrect. In this case, we should look up the canonical name on the original hostname before failing.

Maybe we can change the code to:
   1. look at the redirect URL
   2. check if the URL looks good (currently meaning 'hostname does not begin with www', but we could expand that)
   3a: If the URL is good, continue with that
   3b. If not, look up canonical name

Hi @all, if you are using a proxy behind django app, you may forget to set https/http forward headers on proxy properly.(just like X_FORWARD balabala)

So django will get incorrect URL of your app.

In order to provide a quick fix, I released a new pypi version 2.1.1 last night, which added ASSERTION_URL in SETTINGS. you may want to use it to overwrite the incorrect one which detected automatically by django.

Depend on your http schema, this setting should like
"ASSERTION_URL": "http(s)://your.domain.name"
(No postfix "/")

Adding this for anybody trying to make this type of DB on MacOSX. also for my own future reference when I forget how to do this and need to reinstall. (the docker solution did not work so this is the manual solution that worked for me):

Requirements: 
* Need to have Xcode downloaded for Cmake
* Homebrew

Starters... make sure you have postgresql downloaded for used for 'pg_config'
$ brew install postgresql

INSTRUCTIONS:
download source for postgresql:
https://ftp.postgresql.org/pub/source/v9.6.0/postgresql-9.6.0.tar.bz2
(get the correct version number ___mine is: 10.3)

unzip...

change /contrib/cube/cubedata.h to include 128 dimensions:
#define CUBE_MAX_DIM (100) -> #define CUBE_MAX_DIM (128)

//128 float for facial Encodings

*********
Follow Directions in the 'INSTALL' file at top directory,
Follow instructions for install and for starting server:

./configure
make
su
make install
adduser postgres
mkdir /usr/local/pgsql/data
chown postgres /usr/local/pgsql/data
su - postgres
/usr/local/pgsql/bin/initdb -D /usr/local/pgsql/data

***Start Server:
/usr/local/pgsql/bin/postgres -D /usr/local/pgsql/data

***note
(use this command to change whoami on mac)
$ sudo su - postgres


Now we need to add the extension. go to the /contrib/ directory
follow directions in the README, we can either make all make all install
for all extensions or we can navigate to /contrib/cube/ 
and just: 
$ make 
$ make install
for this one extension

_______________________________________________________________
Now you want to go to your database and add the extension.
For this I just used my GUI and ran the following:
CREATE EXTENSION cube

Dear @gordol and @miguelgrinberg, thank you for your comments!

I think I understand the difference between native threads and green thread quite well, and realize how they can be used together. You are right, using highly computational intensive thread in the process of IO server is not a good idea, but that was not the point, just an example of using native working thread together with the event loop.

My point is there could be quite normal use-cases when developer desires to send a message to socket.io client from a separate (not eventloop) thread. More strictly speaking, submit the function to be invoked from socket.io event loop next time it spins (or, I suspect, when it is going to invoke 'select' function).

Just to make my point clear I mentioned Tornado server implementation. I suppose the implementation of method like Tornado's "IOLoop.add_callback" does not contradict to any of  	fundamental principles behind socket.io server.

I do realize, that typically we could use message broker like Redis or RabbitMQ, and use task managing system like Celery, which I do really like, btw. But there are cases when you are restricted to a single process. And I am dealing with such requirement at the moment. The idea is quite simple, I am writing a desktop application which has socket.io interface (server) and may perform a 1 or 2 long-running jobs in a worker threads. Once job finishes I need to notify socket.io client. That is it. Current implementation contains `while True: eventlet.sleep(...)` kludge, but it a polling and just weird. Tornado's "IOLoop.add_callback" is much elegant solution to allow separate threads to submit arbitrary function (e.g. sending message to client) to the main event loop.

Best regards,
Alexander.

it just occurred to be that maybe it is possible to eliminate the central broker completely. is that what you were referring to with the multi-pub/multi-sub comments?

with the current implementation of ZmqManager, all messages from ZmqManager instances go to the central broker and from there back out to the instances. it may make more sense to have each instance of the ZmqManager send messages out as individual publishers and also receive messages as individual subscribers. If that works (I'm not certain at this point if it will, I'll have to try it), then it would seem that the central broker would not be needed at all. if i'm understanding it correctly, this would completely side-step the need for a robust broker because no broker would be needed. and this would also more closely align with the 'no-broker' philosophy of zmq. in fact, the only reason i used a broker to begin with was because i was modeling the zmq manager after the redis manager. there may be some issues that make this impractical, but i will investigate.

```python
from bcrypt import hashpw, gensalt
from colanderalchemy import setup_schema
from sqlalchemy import Column, DateTime, func, Integer, Unicode
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property

Base = declarative_base()

class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'user'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)

    login = Column(Unicode, nullable=False, unique=True)
    _password = Column('password', Unicode, nullable=False, info={'colanderalchemy': {'name': 'password'}})

    first_name = Column(Unicode, nullable=False)
    last_name = Column(Unicode, nullable=False)

    creation_date = Column(DateTime, default=func.now())

    @hybrid_property
    def password(self):
        return None

    @password.setter
    def password(self, value):
        self._password = hashpw(value.encode('utf-8'), gensalt()).decode('ascii')

    def check_password(self, value):
        return hashpw(value.encode('utf-8'), self._password.encode('ascii')) == self._password.encode('ascii')


setup_schema(None, User)
```

I suspect that your problem is due to the fact that your security document is probably empty and there is no "Cloudant" or "cloudant" field.  One thing to keep in mind is that most of the classes provided by this library are subclasses of `dict` so ultimately they will all behave like a dictionary.  I suggest changing your security document manipulation to something like:

```python
with SecurityDocument(db) as security_document:
    security_document.update({'cloudant': {'nobody': ['_reader']}
```

which will update the security document to add a "cloudant" field and assign the "nobody" `dict` to it.

@sdikby Thanks for the comment! As you mentioned, the previous PR did not handle the case that the target dag has subdag properly, so I revised it.
With the new PR, one can specify either a whole dag or just a subdag. For example, given the following DAG:

```
DAG_P: Task_A >> Task_B >> Task_C (both A and B are SubDagOperator)
Task_A: Task_a1 >> Task_a2
Task_B: Task_b1 >> Task_b2
```

* `airflow remove_dag DAG_P` removes all records related to DAG_P, including the ones related to DAG_P.Task_A and DAG_P.Task_B.
* `airflow remove_dag DAG_P.Task_A` only removes the records related to DAG_P.Task_A and remains the ones related to DAG_P itself and DAG_P.Task_B.
If any questions remaining please move it to the mailinglist

One aspect to keep into consideration is that the order in which the brokers are restarted should be as much as possible stable, because if something goes wrong during the rolling restart, we can use the `--skip` parameter to continue from where we left. This change creates the potential for a non-stable ordering of brokers when the controller changes.

I suggest making this feature optional.

As a side note: it would be nice to implement this change together with an option that will automatically skip brokers restarted withing the last X minutes or hours. In this way we could automatically recover the execution of a rolling restart, and not have to rely on the order of nodes. Thoughts? We could use the jvm uptime provided via jmx.

@frankandrobot 
Your api speicifes the presence of an array parameter named `user_role`.
Checking the traceback I see that you're trying to call the endpoint as `client.users.getUsers(user_role='viewer|publisher|administrator')` which is leading you to the error.
The correct call that you should use should look like ``client.users.getUsers(user_role=['viewer','publisher','administrator'])``

This happens because `marshal_param` method in bravado-core is responsible for converting your array of strings into a single string by joining the components with `|`.

Spoke with @kyleknap and @JordonPhillips about this and there's a few things worth noting:

* We've added a few things to `ResponseMetadata` since this PR was first sent, most notably response headers, and we're concerned about adding more things to `ResponseMetadata`, particularly something like a list of retries.  When we eventually support user configured retries, this number could be potentially large.  There's also no way for a user to opt-out of getting all this extra data.
* The boolean in the response metadata is helpful if you're looking at response metadata.  We were also considering updating the error message in the `ClientError` to specify that the max number of retry attempts was reached so that people just catching and printing `ClientError` would know that max retry attempts were reached.

For people that want more insight into what's being retried, I think it makes sense to update our events so that people can write custom event handlers that can track retry attempts however they see fit, but that would be a separate pull request.

So to summarize:

* remove `Retry` list in response metadata
* update `ClientError` message if max retries were hit to indicate we tried N amount of times.

If those changes seem reasonable, I'll go ahead and pull in your changes, make the updates above, and resend the pull request.

The ZTE only tracks changes done by the ORM itself. If you are doing changes via `session.execute`, etc then the ZTE will not detect them. You need to use `zope.sqlalchemy.mark_changed(session)` to tell it that it changed. Alternatively you can initialize the ZTE into the `changed` state by default so it will always try to do a commit.

my version
```python
def MySchema(Schema):
    thing = UnicodeField()
```

your version
```python
def MySchema(Schema):
    thing = UnicodeField()
```

I like my version because it reads more like the shape of the blob it defines, it's less verbose, and even if it is magic syntax, it's simple magic and not confusing once you know one thing.
```json
{
  "thing": "wow",
  "from": {

}
```

Yes it's possible, but a little convoluted currently.  We don't have any sample code that shows how to do it, but the general process would be something like this (assuming a single datasource):

* Open the workbook
* For each datasource, access the fields property.
* Keep a mapping of worksheets that map to a set
* For each field in the datasource, access the worksheets property and loop over the worksheet names to access the set, and add the field to the set for that worksheet.  You could add a tuple if you wanted to include which datasource it came from.

The worksheets property contains the names of each worksheet where the field is used.

It's convoluted, but workable.

@ChaosMTA Are you using Requests? I think it's this problem: https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/1882

It's being described as "annoying, but not a bug" because it's not so much of an indication of a problem as it is just a client that Requests was keeping open in case you made another requests, and which was disposed of without being explicitly closed.

You should be able to fix it by managing the lifetime of your own `Session` object like this:

``` python
from requests import sessions
session = sessions.Session()
client = stripe.http_client.RequestsClient(session=session)
stripe.default_http_client = client

# do work

session.close()
```

@ThomasWalter I'm not sure whether I get the full meaning of your post but I'm aware of some of the problems from my work on the 1D case in SciPy. Maybe we should first find a definition of how to measure the prominence in n dimensions. 

E.g. a defnition might be: *The prominence is the difference between the height of a maximum M1 and the height of its contour line. Given all lines that encircle a maximum M1 and don't contain a maximum with a larger height than M1, the line with the lowest height is the contour line of M1. For the 3d case the contour line becomes a contour surface, for the 4d case a contour volume, ... (See also [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topographic_prominence))*

I think the watershed algorithm (as @jni suggests) can be used to find these contour lines.

> if a maximum M1 is connected by path to M2 and there is only little decrease on the path, both maxima will have a low prominence.

Using the definition above this is not the case. Assuming that one maximum M1 is larger than the other M2, then the contour line of M1 encircles M2. M2's contour line musn't encirle M1 so both will have a different prominence. However if they have the same height, their contour lines are identical and encircle both maxima.

> The problem is that if you want to suppress maxima that are too close to each other, it is not clear which of the maxima is to be suppressed.

I think we should let the user decide in which order (height, prominence, ...) maxima are removed when satisfying the distance condition. Both approaches have different drawbacks, advantages and applications. So I think it would be useful to make the basic algorithmic building blocks accessible. E.g. the API could look like this (function names are just suggestions):

```python

def local_maxima(...):
    ...

def prominence(image, extrema, ...):
    """
    Calculate prominence of each extrema.
    """
    ...

def select_by_distance(extrema, priority, min_distance, ...):
    """
```

@scottpurdy I just made the changes to support backward compatibility. Here are the changes:
* default value of `numCategories` is set to `1000`, so support a potentially large number of output categories (i.e. inference results of the CLAClassifier). Right now the parameter `numCategories` is required. It's unclear to me if it should be or not. If it's required, it's probably going to impact the ability to run old models checkpoints, right? It might be better to have this param as optional for now.
* updated `customCompute()` so that it does only inference, and not learning. I have extracted the `infer`  part in `CLAClassfier.py` and I am calling this new `infer` function in `customCompute` now. That should prevent the model from learning two times if `customCompute` is called while `compute` has also be called wile running the model. `customCompute` will keep outputting the inference results output it was outputting before. 

I think this should fix the backward compatibility issue. Let me know what you think of that.

Oh, I've told you to build it with `--build-arg DEMO=true` :(

"--build-arg DEMO=true" is used to build an image with postgres 10 only and without a lot of heavy stuff. It is good enough to try it with minikube for example, but it is not for production, because there is wal-e inside.

And you hit a bug that $PATH wasn't propagated to patroni and it failed to run pg_ctl initdb.
Anyway, bug in spilo is fixed and it should work.

Normal spilo image contains postgres 9.3, 9.4, 9.5, 9.6 and 10.
By default it will start postgres 10, but you can change it with environment variable: `SPILO_CONFIGURATION='{postgresql: {bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/9.6/bin}'`
Basically it is possible to supply any part of patroni configuration in the `SPILO_CONFIGURATION` and it would be "merged" into config generated by spilo.

#1 sounds good, but we'll likely have to resort to `setup.py` mod hacks (as we do today internally) to achieve SHA-versioned (but I think it would be worthwhile to have this annotation on the .whl inside the pex). we might be able to improve this with a setuptools plugin, or it may not be worth it.

for #2, a shell script seems fine for now. eventually, I wonder if we couldn't get away with doing this purely in `pants`. `--python-setup-platforms` could be decomposed as 3 separate `BUILD` targets (e.g. `src/python/pants/bin:{pants_osx,pants_linux,pants_multi}`). and `--python-repos-repos` could potentially be done as a simple custom target type (`pants_binary`?) that wrapped `PythonBinary`, parameterized the current git sha as `python_binary(repositories=['http://.../{}/'.format(sha)])` and injected likewise SHA-versioned deps, etc.

it would probably also make sense for the same binary builder shards generating pants' own bdists to also do a `pip wheel` resolve against pants' transitive deps and stash those in the S3 bucket also (i.e. automating the cheeseshop model).. this would 1) ensure that the same machine builds the native_engine binary as well as any backing 3rdparty deps for alignment and 2) enable painless multi-platform resolves (from any runtime OS) for deps like `psutil` that don't provide bdists on pypi etc. otherwise, afaict `pex_from_whls.sh` will never be able to create a singular multi-platform pex since it can only ever run on one platform at a time.

@copumpkin this is a part of shipping the v2 engine as a default in 1.3.0, which is still under active development noted by the version suffix (e.g. `1.3.0.dev18`). dev releases don't carry strong guarantees around behavior changes outside of public API deprecations - and we rely on the testing period for dev releases to catch bugs like this before cutting the 1.3.0 rcs (e.g. `1.3.0rc0`) and ultimately the release (`1.3.0`).

so, if you want stability here you should be tracking stable releases vs dev releases. current stable releases are `1.2.x` now until `1.3.0` goes out.

I was testing the sql-explorer with an table which has about 600000 rows. Since there is no paging in the QueryResult I got timeouts in the browser. So I added the limit. I can see two options to solve the problem:

Hi @KennethNielsen 

I am just wondering if the changes to events.py are really necessary.  The content of each event received is logged at the DEBUG level in any case, so proper configuration of logging can cause just those entries to be recorded. And logging is already thread-safe. We can also use the logging module to be flexible about whether the events are written to the console, to a file, to a file on a remote machine, over a socket etc etc.  Something like this:
``` python
try:
    from Queue import Empty
except ImportError:
    from queue import Empty # Py3

import logging
import soco
from soco.events import event_listener

class EventFilter(object):
    def filter(self, record):
        return 1 if record.msg.startswith("Event content") else 0

# We just want messages from the soco.events module
log = logging.getLogger(('soco.events'))
# And at the DEBUG level
log.setLevel((logging.DEBUG))
# Log to the console (or we could use a FileHandler for a file etc
handler = logging.StreamHandler()
handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
# Filter out messsages that do not start with "Event content"
handler.addFilter(EventFilter())
log.addHandler(handler)

# Everything that follows is just boilerplate to get some events
# pick a device
device = soco.SoCo('192.168.1.102')  # <----- your IP here
print(device.player_name)

sub = device.avTransport.subscribe()
while True:
    try:
        event = sub.events.get(timeout=0.5)
    except Empty:
        pass
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        sub.unsubscribe()
        event_listener.stop()
        break
```

PTAL @vivianho

1. As far as I know `Service-Worker-Allowed` header is only necessary for the script resource (the server worker file) when the scope is to be customized. This is only utilized when the service worker is installed. Once the installation is done and scope is registered properly in the browser, the role of this header is over. No other resources need to have those headers. In fact it does not even make any sense because the fetch events need to be trapped at request time (before any response is received) also the resources might be hosted on a different domain where setting a custom header wont make sense.
2. If registration is causing an issue with custom scope, then we can begin with hosting the file in the root, but perhaps it would be more important to find out why it is failing. You might want to leverage [the examples of setting up custom scope](https://w3c.github.io/ServiceWorker/#service-worker-script-response).
3. Once the script is installed, only fetch event should be triggered when other resources in the scope are requested. Install and register events will only be fired when the page that registers the service worker is loaded.

I have a different opinion here. The script is not deploying anything, it is releasing a new version instead. That said, it does not have the timezone information in it, so it could be problematic in some situations. Suppose, I visit India (which happens to be 9.5 or 10.5 hours ahead of the Eastern Time) and I publish a new version by running that script. A few minutes later you make some changes and push another release using the same script. This last version number is going to have an earlier time (and perhaps a day earlier too). Even if that situation is far fetched, it illustrates the problem of not making it timezone-neutral. These version numbers are for users, which we don't expect them to have the same TZ as ours or know that which TZ the code was released form.

Never mind, I figured this one out, however the errors messages weren't helping :) , for documentation purposes only, the problem is the way I formatted the dates in the recurrence (ScheduleTriggerRecurrence object)

python isoformat() does not include the UTC offset (-08:00, -04:00, etc.) 

I had to add the UTC offset and voila!

for example:

```
        str_start_date = '2017-10-31T17:17:00-04:00'
        str_end_date = '2099-12-31T05:30:00-04:00'
```

This post also helped me to get there:

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19654578/python-utc-datetime-objects-iso-format-doesnt-include-z-zulu-or-zero-offset

I'm still curious to see how to use the time_zone argument as I was originally using 'UTC', for now I removed it and hard-coded the UTC offset.

>Maybe then we should have one efficient Einsum adjoint routine which is used by all of the linear operators, and that ought to have pretty good performance for everything and would be less code.

I've been thinking a bit more about this today. I've noticed there's a (fairly obvious) relationship between the JOs and the JTOs which can be described something like this... 

The JO of a primitive `fun`, with one argument (denoted `x`),  will always have the form
```
JO(fun)(x)(v) = bilinear_operator(deriv(x), v)
```
where deriv is some function of `x` (and sometimes in practice also of `ans = fun(x)`) and `bilinear_operator` is a function of two arguments which is linear in it's first (second) argument holding it's second (first) argument constant. For example, for unary ufuncs `bilinear_operator` is often just `lambda x, v: x * v`, but sometimes it's more complicated and is expressed as a `dot`, `tensordot` or `einsum`.

Using the above notation, the JTO of `fun` is (I think) always of the form
```
JTO(fun)(x)(v) = transpose(bilinear_operator)(deriv(x), v)
```
where `transpose` is a function which (somehow) transposes the bilinear operator w.r.t. it's second argument, for example it would map `tensordot` to `tensordot_adjoint_1` from this pr. Note that `deriv(x)` is unchanged, so perhaps we could (and should) separate the definition of `deriv` from the specification of `bilinear_operator`. That way `deriv` could be shared between the JO and the JTO. Furthermore, we could use einsum notation to specify `bilinear_operator`, which we know how to transpose, since we already do it in the vjp of einsum.

I think we could save ourselves from separately specifying jvps and vjps in this way. We would need to think about how to do `transpose` efficiently. This could potentially make implementing https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/pull/280 a little more straightforward too, by isolating the changes needed in `binary_operator` and `transpose`. There might be issues with this approach. I haven't thought yet about whether it would work ok for complex derivatives.

Good start thanks.  About your last point, I think modularizing is very important for parsing the config, and am skeptical that YAML (which I know nothing about) provides enough of it.  Things like:
* loading up just the mapping options in one go (and generally dividing the config into blocks)
* scraping all the docker tools into an array
* specifiying variable numbers of chained commands for the indexing
* re-using the same template for, say, cores/disk/memory in many places without manually reparsing. 
* automatically converting to relevant types and checking validity

JSON and XML (I lean towards JSON here just because its prevalence in vg) both support the above natively.  I'm not married to JSON but what I see here doesn't convince me YAML is as good. 

Couple other general points:
* Please stick the default config in its own text (or .py file if that's easier) file.  For generation, just dump it to stdout.  
* Leave options that are going to change all the time as command line options.  Like input files, output stores and toil parameters, path names.  
* Focus on the vg parameters that are currently hardcoded.  Current example just contains existing command line options, and don't  address the root cause of this issue (not being able to tune mapping)
* OK to leave sets of options grouped together in strings (like being done for call_opts etc), at least for now.  Would propose getting rid of some options that violate this. (like kmer size having its own first class option).  
* Please add docker entry points to the docker tools options (so they are tool/entrypoint pairs), so we don't have to hardcode anymore. 
* Keep in mind all disk usage / memory / and cores for all toil jobs should eventually be somehow derived from these options (as opposed to hardcoded like they mostly are).

Reg. electron: There are already some apps out there using electron in combination with .net core - e.g. https://catlight.io

A lot of popular modern applications are built around electron, even MS products like VS Code.

I'm not sure that trying to compete with Electron makes more sense than trying to use it as a platform. There is so much experimental and proof-of-concept work going on with running Xamarin.Forms on the web via WebAssembly (+ upcoming DOM APIs for WebAssembly), or making ASP.NET Core / Razor run on WebAssembly (Blazor project), all of which would leverage the effort that continuously goes into Browsers and Browser-based shells.
That also allows to instantly share components used in web applications with native apps, even if it is just a React/Angular/* framework calling to a .net core backend running locally.
I don't really think that adding another platform trying to compete with the shift towards web technologies as cross-platform UI stack is worth the engineering effort.

On the other hand, allowing existing desktop applications to run on CoreCLR and make use of the deployment strategies introduced by .NET Core (side-by-side / self-contained) makes sense since it likely is a low engineering effort (still significant but low compared to creating a new UI stack) to port existing WinForms / XAML code to .NET Core while acknowledging that its use of Windows-only APIs restricts its use to Windows. But that instantly gives maintained existing Applications the chance to make use of new features both from .NET Core but also long stating issues in the desktop frameworks that can't really be solved without a few breaking changes so they can never be fixed in an update to .NET Framework  (high-DPI support and "XAML Islands" is being mentioned frequently for this).

Hi @grepwood,

Thanks for the question.

This kind of question is more appropriate for the Let's Encrypt Community Forum at https://community.letsencrypt.org/ (where organizational and policy questions about ISRG services are on-topic). This particular issue tracker is only intended for technical problems with the Python client software.

There have been some previous discussions on the community forum that are somewhat related to your question, although I don't think anyone phrased it in exactly the same terms.

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/lets-encrypt-and-u-s-laws/3251/1

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/what-is-the-business-model/310

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/ssl-is-broken-the-backdoor-is-built-in/878

I've tried to address some of these concerns in forum posts. The short answer is that every CA has policy and technical controls that are meant to discourage it from misissuing certs (or inappropriately disclosing its own keys), but auditors can't watch day-to-day to ensure that CAs don't subvert their own control mechanisms. So, right now we're reliant on auditors' determination that a CA's controls and intentions are credible, and on browser vendors and the information security community to monitor what CAs really do in practice and detect and investigate instances of misissuance. Initiatives like Certificate Transparency (which Let's Encrypt has supported from the outset) aim to increase the transparency of CA operations and reduce CAs' ability to undetectably do the wrong thing.

@lukas2511 is right to point out that, given the design of TLS, the CA (even if malicious) never has direct access to user's encrypted communications. The worst thing that the CA could do is allow other parties (who control or tamper with network infrastructure) to perform an active attack against some communications at a particular time, which may not be automatically detected or prevented by the browser, but which does run some risk of leaving behind publicly-visible evidence.

Please feel free to continue this discussion on the community forum if you'd like.

Adding support for [KIP-62](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/KIP-62%3A+Allow+consumer+to+send+heartbeats+from+a+background+thread) is probably the way to go here.

> Decoupling the processing timeout: We propose to introduce a separate locally enforced timeout for record processing and a background thread to keep the session active until this timeout expires. We call this new timeout as the "process timeout" and expose it in the consumer's configuration as max.poll.interval.ms. This config sets the maximum delay between client calls to poll(). When the timeout expires, the consumer will stop sending heartbeats and send an explicit LeaveGroup request. As soon as the consumer resumes processing with another call to poll(), the consumer will rejoin the group. This is equivalent to the current processing model except that it allows the user to set a higher timeout when processing while also using a lower session timeout for faster crash detection.

`poll()` should only return `None` when it times out waiting for a message or event, but since you are not specifying a timeout it should block until a proper message or event is received.
This might indicate a bug in the client, but first I want you to correct the None checking, change your code to check for `message_object is not None` and `message object is None` accordingly.
Thus:
```
...
while running:
  message_object = kafka_consumer.poll()
  if message_object is None:
    print('poll timeout, weird..')
  elif not message_object.error():
    print('Received message: %s' % message_object.value().decode('utf-8'))
    ... do processing
  elif message_object.error().code() != KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
      print(message_object.error())
      running = False
  else:
    print("Reached EOF for %s" % message_object)
```

@jrosebr1 I'm sorry about these issues. Thanks so much for such a detailed account, that is really useful! I am setting up a script to reproduce this now thanks to all your scripts and the dataset. However, I do think I know what's going on already. As part of the a pre-processing step, the object detector takes all images in the SFrame and decodes them. This is to avoid the overhead of constantly decoding them. However, your images are large and some take up to 23 MB uncompressed. The way the object detector uses the SFrame is not optimal, since normally out-of-memory datasets are well handled. This is definitely something I want to see improved in the object detector.

As a work-around before this has addressed, you can try to scale down your images a bit first. They will be scaled down before they are passed into the neural network anyway, so it won't make a difference for the model performance. You can do something like this to get the size down:

```
sf['image'] = sf['image'].apply(lambda image: tc.image_analysis.resize(image, image.width//4, image.height//4, image.channels))
```

Let me know if that works as a temporary work-around.

def get_output_for(self, input, deterministic=False, **kwargs):
        
        i0 = input[:,:,0::2,0::2]
        i1 = input[:,:,0::2,1::2]
        i2 = input[:,:,1::2,0::2]
        i3 = input[:,:,1::2,1::2]
        
        new_shape = (i0.shape[0],i0.shape[1],i0.shape[2]*i0.shape[3])
        res_i0 = i0.reshape(new_shape)
        res_i1 = i1.reshape(new_shape)
        res_i2 = i2.reshape(new_shape)
        res_i3 = i3.reshape(new_shape)
               
        wones  = T.ones((new_shape[1],new_shape[2]))
        w0     = T.dot(self.W[:,0], wones)
        w1     = T.dot(self.W[:,1], wones)
        w2     = T.dot(self.W[:,2], wones)
        w3     = T.dot(self.W[:,3], wones)
    
        res_out = res_i0*w0 + res_i1*w1 + res_i2*w2 + res_i3*w3
        
        out = res_out.reshape((input.shape[0],self.output_shape[1],self.output_shape[2],self.output_shape[3]))
        return out  `

> 'end of the world' is a bit strong indeed, but it is a bit of a wart imo. We should avoid forcing users to use things in unintuitive ways, which I think this is an instance of.

+1

> I think the `BatchIterator` stuff might already go a bit too far. What benefits does this abstraction offer over just working with Python generators?

So if you want to support a `fit()` method that takes the entire dataset (such as it's often done in sklearn), you'll need to split it into batches, and that's what `BatchIterator` is for.  The good news is a lot of times you don't need to worry about it, it'll do the simple thing of generating batches of given (default) size in the background.

If what you want is test-time augmentation, which I often use, you subclass.  Here's an example:

```python
class MyBatchIterator(BatchIterator):
    def transform(self, X, y):
        return X + 1, y  # silly example always adds 1 to X
```

I think this is an example of an okay pattern that you can easily remember.  For some users it's arguably easier 'filling in the blanks' as opposed to taking control over train loop, batch generation etc.  For the advanced user, it might seem restrictive.  It guess it's basically framework versus library.

But certainly, if you're striving for one way to do this, and you want to have control over the train loop yourself, then I guess you'd probably get rid of `fit()` altogether and only provide the equivalent of `partial_fit()`?

> Maybe we should strive to merge it (or some derivative of it) into Lasagne proper, and fix the docs in the process?

Would be happy to try that.  It would be a good opportunity to clean things up, too.  That said, I'm not sure it'll be easy to get a consensus on many things.  You're making a good point about not forcing users to shoehorn their use cases; it's hard anticipating all the things people might want to do.

is there any update on this? Consider the following example, inspired by @mwilliamson-healx, where 100K `Users` need to be returned. Using graphene.Scalar the response is `x8` faster.
 

- Is there any way to drop the type checking/casting? Or anyway to speed up the resolves? 
It is not really a use case to return such number of objects however it is a use case to return a few thousand nested objects.

```python
import graphene
import cProfile
import StringIO
import pstats
from contextlib import contextmanager
from graphene.test import Client

@contextmanager
def profile(show_calls=None, message=None):
  print("\n============== Profiler start ==============" )
  if message:
    print(" " + message + " ... ")

  pr = cProfile.Profile()
  pr.enable()
  yield
  pr.disable()
  s = StringIO.StringIO()
  sortby = 'cumulative'
  ps = pstats.Stats(pr, stream=s).sort_stats(sortby)
  if show_calls:
    ps.print_stats()
    print s.getvalue()
  else:
    print ('Execution time %f seconds' % ps.total_tt)
  print("--------------------------------------------")


class UserQuery(graphene.ObjectType):
    id = graphene.Int()

class UserAbstract(graphene.Scalar):

  @staticmethod
  def serialize(dt):
    return dt

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    users = graphene.Field(graphene.List(UserQuery))
    users_abstract = graphene.Field(graphene.List(UserAbstract))

    def resolve_users(self, context):
      return users

    def resolve_users_abstract(self, context):
      return resolved_users


class User(object):
    def __init__(self, id):
        self.id = id

nof_users = 100000
users = [User(index) for index in range(nof_users)]
resolved_users = [user.__dict__ for user in users]

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)

with profile(message="Fetch using ObjectType", show_calls=False):
  response = Client(schema).execute('{users{id}}')
  assert (len(response['data']['users']) == nof_users)


with profile(message="Fetch using Scalar", show_calls=False):
  response = Client(schema).execute('{usersAbstract}')
  assert (len(response['data']['usersAbstract']) == nof_users)
```

```
 Fetch using ObjectType ... 
Execution time 8.595509 seconds
--------------------------------------------

============== Profiler start ==============
 Fetch using Scalar ... 
Execution time 1.008313 seconds
--------------------------------------------
```

The plotting by site issue was caused by having the wrong default for isite.  However, once I fixed that I got into the *real* mess, which is different than I was expecting.

What we want for ipmag.aniso_magic is to take in some arguments, quietly create few plots, and then return them.  However, pmagplotlib is very much written to produce plots interactively with matplotlib.  The code is full of calls to plt.show, etc., and pmagplotlib never actually returns Figure objects, it just shows or saves them.  This is a huge mess for the notebooks (or the GUIs, for that matter).  matplotlib has an object-oriented API that for creating plots without all the interactive display logic, but pmagplotlib would need to be re-written.

ipmag.ani_depthplot and ipmag.core_depthplot work in a more object-oriented way, but that's because I re-wrote them to go in the GUIs, and they don't really use pmagplotlib.  We're going to run into problems with most of the rest of the plotting programs, though.

We could consider rewriting pmagplotlib anyway, so that it would be useable for web PmagPy.  On the other hand, if we're rewriting we might want to consider other tools that could provide more interactivity online, like plotly.

I am referring to the `FFTW` class. It was clear that the API is not meant to resemble numpy or scipy but maybe the statement "This argument is equivalent to the same argument in numpy.fft.fftn()" lead me to my false assumptions.

This is great work and a really valuable contribution. As it stands though, there are a few things that concern me before merging:

1) The tests are rather sparse. In particular, AFAICT, there are no tests at all for the new builder functions. The tests you have included test the functionality of the new code but very little in the way of testing failure modes and edge cases. I appreciate this is a huge pain to do completely, but the effort on the existing code base has proved to yield a very robust almost bug-free library (I think there is one proper bug in the issue list at the moment). My strategy would have been to re-purpose as much of the existing testing infrastructure as possible (this is how all the other transforms are tested, and I don't think r2r is so radically different) - the classes were designed very much with that in mind. Also, those tests should be manually validated as failing when the functionality is removed.

As an aside, I've actually picked up more than one bug in FFTW itself through the thorough testing.

2) I think we really need to move across to the API discussed in #39 - specifically, deprecate the `direction` parameter and use a `transform_type` argument with suitable enumerations. `direction` is IMO a confusing misnomer when it comes to r2r (and has been for a long time anyway). This would improve `_Xfftn` as well.

3) The interfaces bit should be complete before merging, and as close as possible to `scipy.fftpack`. `scipy.fftpack.dct(a, type=1, norm='ortho')` fails with `NotImplementedError`, but `pyfftw.interfaces.scipy_fftpack.dct(a, type=1, norm='ortho')` will quietly ignore the norm flag. This should also be acknowledged in the test suite.

Similarly, the `n` parameter not `None` raises. Can the existing `_FFTWWrapper` not be modified quite easily? (it just wraps the `FFTW` object with suitable slicers).

4) The documentation needs updating.

As I said, this is valuable work so I sincerely encourage you to persist with this! Thanks!

The way I first implemented it was something like this: (I changed it around so that I removed company private code and this was way before I started contributing to dask so my knowledge was far less than it is today)

import numpy as np

import dask.bag as db



from toolz.curried import frequencies

from toolz.curried import valmap

from toolz.curried import merge_with

from toolz.curried import unique

from toolz import merge_with as _merge_with





def normalize(_dict):

    return {k:v/sum(_dict.values()) for k,v in _dict.items()}



corpus = 2*[

    "a a b b b",

    "a a a a c",

    "c b a d e f g",

    "a b b b c c c d d d a b",

    "c b a d e f g",

    "a b b b c c c d d d a b"

]





base = (

    db

    .from_sequence(corpus, npartitions=4)

    .map(str.split)

)



tf = (

    base

    .map(frequencies)

    .map(normalize)

)



idf = (

    base

    .map(unique)

    .map(frequencies)

    .reduction(merge_with(sum), merge_with(sum), split_every=2)

    .apply(normalize)

    .apply(valmap(np.log10))

    .apply(valmap(np.negative))

)

This has a draw-back, since it must pass over the entire dataset once in order to calculate the idf step.

To me a better solution (which is what I want to implement here) would be first to implement the hashing trick<http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.HashingVectorizer.html#sklearn.feature_extraction.text.HashingVectorizer> and then use the tfidf<http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer.html#sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer>.

1. Not all config options are currently specified in either place. Listing descriptions of config options as seen in `IConfig` should be done there rather than in `NamespaceConfig`.
2. The type would be `list of <type>` where `<type>` is the type of the object contained in the list. Here's an example for [domains](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/686fa36b3b8eea3ed8859c6af9d0acd7ee9b8978/certbot/hooks.py#L158) and a somewhat poorly documented example for [plugins](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/686fa36b3b8eea3ed8859c6af9d0acd7ee9b8978/certbot/plugins/selection.py#L101).
3. `authenticator` and `installer` change which class they are referring to, but they always implement `certbot.interfaces.IAuthenticator` and `certbot.interfaces.IInstaller` respectively. `lineage` should refer to `certbot.storage.RenewableCert`.
4. In Python, functions are first class objects. Here's a brief [stackoverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/245192/what-are-first-class-objects) describing what that means, but basically it means that functions can be assigned to variables, passed to functions, returned from other functions, etc. [Here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/686fa36b3b8eea3ed8859c6af9d0acd7ee9b8978/certbot/cli.py#L563) `func` is set to the function to call for the subcommand requested by the user.

I hope that makes sense! Feel free to ask any other questions that you have.

Relatedly, if you're planning on digging through Certbot and carefully looking at types, I'd like to encourage you to also look at #4244. This issue is about specifying the types used in Certbot in a way that another program called `mypy` can understand to help us verify that Certbot behaves correctly. For example, if we document that the function takes an `int` and someone calls it with a string, `mypy` can warn us of this problem when vanilla Python won't.

There are a couple options we have here which should be discussed:

1. **How do we structure this in the main menu?**
    1. **Adding a "Graphical User Interface" entry directly below the "Command-Line Interface" entry.**  
        This would somehow divide the documentation from the remaining main menu entries. And third party CLI programs using Streamlink would not fit here.
    2. **Adding a "Third Party Software" entry to the bottom.**  
        This solves the issue mention above.
2. **How do we list different kind of programs?**
    1. **Use one single list**  
        Can become confusing when there are many applications listed in no order. (See the livestreamer wiki page)
    2. **Group by CLI and GUI applications**  
        Makes more sense for people who are looking for those kind of programs, but disadvantages programs not listed at the top.
    3. **Split CLI and GUI applications and use sub menus**
    4. **Order applications by name or by popularity / usefulness / quality**  
        Who will decide this?
3. **What to display in these lists?**
    - Name
    - URL
    - Short or long descriptions? (expandable?!)
    - Preview images?
4. **Do we want to build this page automatically (from a json file or so) or should it be static?**  
    Multiple PRs from different people with possibly different markup can make things inconsistent, so building this page would be better than using plain html.

which I believe is the output from https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/blob/master/dedupe/blocking.py#L42

As so far we have enough memory, I had to change the code here to let the blocking happen in a 
dictionary in memory :
https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/blob/master/dedupe/api.py#L1072

Basically, instead of returning `shelf`, return an empty python dictionary:
```python
def _temp_shelve():
    fd, file_path = tempfile.mkstemp()
    os.close(fd)

    try:
        shelf = shelve.open(file_path, 'n',
                                      protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
    except Exception as e:
        if 'db type could not be determined' in str(e):
            os.remove(file_path)
            shelf = shelve.open(file_path, 'n',
                                protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
        else:
            raise

    return {}, file_path # return python dictionary instead of shelf
```
This will make the blocking and matching process takes lots of memory but it can finish a 2 entries matching against 30K records in a few seconds.

Two closely related improvement ideas I've been thinking about:
* Limit the number of returned items by default. To 1000 items, perhaps. It's not difficult to query a large portion of the whole SciHub by accident and we currently do not provide feedback on the number of found products before they are returned. We should print a warning when that limit is in effect, of course. An even lower limit of 100 might be even better to make it more obvious that there's a limit in place by default.
* Add a way to get the number of products matching a query on the server via the API. Something like `query(..., return_count=True)`, which would then return a count of matching products in addition to the products dictionary. The CLI should always print the number of found products to the log anyway.

What do you think guys?
I strongly support both of these suggestions.
